agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Payment processing service with customer-managed encryption keys (CMK), 50ms P99 latency, full field-level audit, offline resilience, and zero-downtime key rotation. The core architectural challenge is that requirements 1, 2, and 4 are in direct tension: true CMK with remote KMS cannot meet 50ms P99 while also working offline. The design resolves this via a local key cache with encrypted Data Encryption Keys (DEKs) using an envelope encryption pattern, async audit pipeline, and multi-generation key support for seamless rotation.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Payment Gateway","responsibility":"Accepts inbound payment requests, validates input schema, rate-limits, and routes to Payment Processor. Stateless.","boundaries":"No access to encryption keys. No persistence. Only schema validation and routing."},{"name":"Payment Processor","responsibility":"Orchestrates the payment lifecycle: validates business rules, calls Encryption Service for field-level encrypt/decrypt, persists via Payment Repository, emits domain events.","boundaries":"Owns the payment aggregate. Does not manage keys directly — delegates to Encryption Service."},{"name":"Encryption Service","responsibility":"Envelope encryption engine. Manages local DEK cache, encrypts/decrypts payment fields using cached DEKs, and wraps/unwraps DEKs via KMS Adapter. Tracks key generations for rotation.","boundaries":"Owns the encryption boundary. Never exposes plaintext DEKs outside its process. Provides encrypt(field, context) / decrypt(ciphertext, context) interface only."},{"name":"KMS Adapter","responsibility":"Anti-corruption layer to external KMS (AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, HashiCorp Vault, etc.). Handles CMK operations: DEK wrapping/unwrapping, key rotation triggers, health checks.","boundaries":"Only component that communicates with external KMS. Abstracts provider differences. Returns wrapped/unwrapped DEKs to Encryption Service."},{"name":"Local Key Cache","responsibility":"In-memory + encrypted-on-disk cache of unwrapped DEKs with TTL. Enables offline operation and sub-millisecond encryption. Stores multiple key generations for rotation overlap.","boundaries":"Encrypted at rest with a local master key derived from HSM or secure enclave. Cache entries have TTL, generation ID, and usage counters."},{"name":"Audit Pipeline","responsibility":"Captures field-level access events asynchronously via a ring buffer. Batches and flushes to durable audit log store. Guarantees at-least-once delivery.","boundaries":"Write-only from service perspective. Append-only log. No audit data flows back into payment processing path."},{"name":"Payment Repository","responsibility":"Persists encrypted payment records. Stores ciphertext + key generation metadata (never plaintext).","boundaries":"Receives only encrypted field values. Stores key generation ID alongside each encrypted field for decryption routing."},{"name":"Key Rotation Coordinator","responsibility":"Background process that triggers key rotation: generates new DEK generation, re-wraps with new CMK version, manages read-drain of old generation, and coordinates re-encryption of at-rest data.","boundaries":"Operates asynchronously. Never blocks payment processing. Old keys remain readable until all data is re-encrypted."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Payment Gateway","to":"Payment Processor","contract":"ProcessPaymentCommand { amount, currency, card_data, idempotency_key } → PaymentResult { id, status, encrypted_receipt }","coupling_type":"synchronous, loose (command/result)"},{"from":"Payment Processor","to":"Encryption Service","contract":"encrypt(field_name, plaintext, context) → EncryptedField { ciphertext, key_generation_id, iv } | decrypt(EncryptedField, context) → plaintext","coupling_type":"synchronous, tight (in-process call, latency-critical)"},{"from":"Encryption Service","to":"Local Key Cache","contract":"get_dek(generation_id?) → DEK | None, put_dek(generation_id, DEK, ttl)","coupling_type":"synchronous, tight (in-memory)"},{"from":"Encryption Service","to":"KMS Adapter","contract":"unwrap_dek(wrapped_dek, cmk_id) → DEK | wrap_dek(dek, cmk_id) → WrappedDEK | rotate_cmk(cmk_id) → new_cmk_version","coupling_type":"synchronous with circuit breaker, medium coupling"},{"from":"Payment Processor","to":"Audit Pipeline","contract":"emit(AuditEvent { timestamp, actor, field_name, operation, payment_id, context }) — fire-and-forget into ring buffer","coupling_type":"asynchronous, loose (ring buffer decouples producer/consumer)"},{"from":"Payment Processor","to":"Payment Repository","contract":"save(EncryptedPayment) → id | find(id) → EncryptedPayment","coupling_type":"synchronous, loose (repository pattern)"},{"from":"Key Rotation Coordinator","to":"Encryption Service","contract":"register_new_generation(generation_id, wrapped_dek) | deprecate_generation(generation_id)","coupling_type":"asynchronous, loose (event-driven)"},{"from":"Key Rotation Coordinator","to":"Payment Repository","contract":"stream_records_by_generation(old_gen) → Stream<EncryptedPayment> for background re-encryption","coupling_type":"asynchronous batch, loose"}],"data_flow":"1. Request arrives at Payment Gateway → validates schema → forwards to Payment Processor. 2. Payment Processor calls Encryption Service.encrypt() for each sensitive field (PAN, CVV, cardholder name). 3. Encryption Service checks Local Key Cache for current-generation DEK. Cache HIT (fast path, <0.1ms): encrypts field with AES-256-GCM using cached DEK. Cache MISS: calls KMS Adapter to unwrap DEK (adds ~5-20ms first time only, then cached). KMS UNAVAILABLE (offline path): uses last-known cached DEK if within TTL, otherwise falls back to offline emergency key sealed in secure enclave. 4. Encrypted fields + key_generation_id stored via Payment Repository. 5. Audit event emitted to ring buffer for every field access (encrypt or decrypt). 6. Audit Pipeline consumer drains ring buffer → batches → writes to append-only audit store. 7. Key Rotation Coordinator runs on 30-day schedule: creates new DEK generation, registers with Encryption Service, background re-encrypts old records, retires old generation only when fully drained.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Envelope Encryption","rationale":"Separates key hierarchy: CMK (in KMS) wraps DEKs (used locally). DEKs can be cached in-memory for speed while CMK stays in KMS for compliance. This is the standard pattern for meeting both the CMK requirement and the latency requirement."},{"pattern":"Circuit Breaker (on KMS Adapter)","rationale":"KMS unavailability must not block payments (requirement 4). Circuit breaker detects KMS failures, trips to open state, and Encryption Service falls back to cached DEKs."},{"pattern":"Multi-Generation Key Support","rationale":"Key rotation (requirement 5) without downtime requires multiple active key generations. New writes use the latest generation; old ciphertext is readable with the old generation until background re-encryption completes."},{"pattern":"Async Audit via Ring Buffer","rationale":"Field-level audit logging (requirement 3) on the hot path would blow the 50ms budget. A lock-free ring buffer (e.g., LMAX Disruptor pattern) decouples audit emission (<1μs) from durable persistence."},{"pattern":"Anti-Corruption Layer (KMS Adapter)","rationale":"Isolates the payment domain from KMS provider specifics. Swapping from AWS KMS to Vault requires changing only the adapter."},{"pattern":"Repository Pattern","rationale":"Payment Repository abstracts storage. Encrypted fields are opaque to the repository — it stores ciphertext without knowledge of keys."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Payment Processing","classification":"core","aggregates":["Payment (root: PaymentId, entities: PaymentAttempt, value objects: Money, CardToken, EncryptedField)"]},{"name":"Cryptographic Operations","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["KeyGeneration (root: GenerationId, value objects: WrappedDEK, KeyMetadata, TTL)","EncryptionOperation (stateless service, no aggregate — pure functions over DEKs)"]},{"name":"Audit","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["AuditTrail (root: TrailId, entities: AuditEntry, value objects: FieldAccessRecord, ActorContext)"]},{"name":"Key Management","classification":"generic","aggregates":["ExternalKMS (abstracted behind KMS Adapter — no domain aggregate, this is infrastructure)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Key Management (external KMS)","downstream":"Cryptographic Operations","relationship":"Anti-Corruption Layer — KMS Adapter translates external KMS API into domain-friendly DEK operations. Downstream is never polluted by KMS provider specifics."},{"upstream":"Payment Processing","downstream":"Audit","relationship":"Published Language — Payment Processing emits AuditEvents in a defined schema. Audit context consumes them without coupling to payment internals."},{"upstream":"Cryptographic Operations","downstream":"Payment Processing","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Payment Processing depends on Crypto for encrypt/decrypt. Crypto team must meet latency SLA. Payment Processing defines what it needs; Crypto delivers."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Cache unwrapped DEKs in local memory for latency","gains":"Sub-millisecond encrypt/decrypt, meets 50ms P99, enables offline operation","sacrifices":"Unwrapped key material exists in process memory — larger attack surface than pure KMS-only model. Requires secure memory handling (mlock, no swap, zeroing on eviction).","alternatives_considered":["Call KMS for every operation — secure but 10-30ms per call, fails offline, cannot meet P99","Hardware Security Module (HSM) co-located — most secure but expensive, still 1-5ms per operation, complex deployment"]},{"decision":"Async audit via ring buffer instead of synchronous durable logging","gains":"Near-zero latency impact on payment path (<1μs to enqueue). Meets 50ms P99 budget.","sacrifices":"Small window of potential audit loss if process crashes before flush (ring buffer is in-memory). Mitigated by: short flush intervals (100ms), WAL-backed ring buffer option.","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous write-ahead log — durable but adds 2-10ms per field access, multiple fields per payment = budget blown","Synchronous append to local file — fast but still 0.5-1ms per field, and file I/O jitter risks P99"]},{"decision":"Multi-generation keys with background re-encryption for rotation","gains":"Zero-downtime rotation. New payments immediately use new key. Old data re-encrypted in background.","sacrifices":"Complexity: must track generation ID per encrypted field. Storage overhead during transition (old + new). Re-encryption backfill can take hours for large datasets.","alternatives_considered":["Big-bang re-encryption in maintenance window — simpler but violates requirement 5","Lazy re-encryption on read — eventually consistent but audit shows mixed generations, and cold data never gets rotated"]},{"decision":"Offline emergency key sealed in secure enclave","gains":"Payments process even during extended KMS outages (requirement 4)","sacrifices":"The offline key is not customer-managed in the purest sense — it is pre-provisioned. Requires clear customer agreement on this degraded mode. Key material in enclave is harder to rotate.","alternatives_considered":["Reject payments when KMS is offline — simplest but violates requirement 4","Long TTL on cached DEKs only — works for short outages but a 24h+ outage with cache eviction would halt payments"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"KMS Provider","evolution_path":"KMS Adapter is the sole integration point. Swap AWS KMS → HashiCorp Vault → Azure Key Vault by implementing a new adapter. No changes to Encryption Service or Payment Processor."},{"area":"Encryption Algorithm","evolution_path":"Encryption Service encapsulates algorithm choice (AES-256-GCM today). Can migrate to AES-256-GCM-SIV or post-quantum algorithms by changing the Encryption Service internals. Ciphertext format includes algorithm identifier for backward compatibility."},{"area":"Audit Store","evolution_path":"Audit Pipeline consumer is pluggable. Today: append-only DB. Tomorrow: S3 + Athena, or SIEM integration. Producer (ring buffer emit) is unchanged."},{"area":"Payment Storage Engine","evolution_path":"Repository pattern abstracts storage. Can migrate from PostgreSQL → DynamoDB → NewSQL without touching encryption or business logic."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Envelope Encryption Pattern","reason":"This is the foundational architectural decision. Changing from envelope encryption to a different key hierarchy would require redesigning the Encryption Service, cache strategy, rotation coordinator, and all stored ciphertext. This is deliberately rigid — it's the right pattern and should not change."},{"area":"Field-Level Encryption Granularity","reason":"Each sensitive field is individually encrypted with its own IV and generation metadata. Moving to record-level encryption later would require data migration and changes to the audit model. Field-level is the right choice for PCI compliance and selective decryption."},{"area":"Audit Event Schema","reason":"The audit event format is a published contract consumed by compliance systems. Changes require versioning and backward compatibility. Schema should be designed carefully upfront."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Cached DEK in memory is exposed if process memory is compromised (cold boot attack, memory dump)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use mlock() to prevent swapping. Zero memory on DEK eviction. Run in a TEE (Trusted Execution Environment) or use SGX enclaves if available. Limit DEK cache TTL to minimize exposure window."},{"risk":"Audit ring buffer data loss on process crash — field accesses between last flush and crash are lost","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Configure flush interval to 100ms maximum. Use memory-mapped ring buffer with WAL semantics for crash recovery. Accept that a 100ms window of audit loss may occur — document this in compliance posture."},{"risk":"50ms P99 budget is extremely tight when accounting for network, serialization, DB write, and encryption","severity":"high","mitigation":"Budget breakdown: Gateway routing ~2ms, Encryption (cached) ~0.5ms per field × 3 fields = ~1.5ms, Business logic ~1ms, DB write ~10-15ms, Audit emit ~0.01ms, Serialization + network ~5ms. Total ~25ms typical, leaving ~25ms headroom. Monitor P99 aggressively. Pre-warm key cache on startup."},{"risk":"Offline mode with pre-provisioned keys may not satisfy strict CMK compliance interpretations","severity":"critical","mitigation":"This is a requirement conflict that must be resolved with the customer and compliance team. True CMK means the customer controls the key — but if KMS is down, the customer cannot assert control. Options: (A) customer pre-authorizes an offline key sealed in their HSM, (B) accept that offline mode uses a degraded security posture with compensating controls, (C) re-negotiate requirement 4 to allow graceful degradation (queue payments, process when KMS returns)."},{"risk":"Key rotation re-encryption backfill may cause elevated DB load and latency spikes","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Throttle re-encryption to N records/second. Run during low-traffic periods (even without a maintenance window). Use separate read replica for scanning old records. Monitor P99 during backfill and auto-throttle if approaching budget."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1: Implement Encryption Service with envelope encryption, local DEK cache, and single KMS adapter (e.g., AWS KMS). Validate 50ms P99 with load testing. Phase 2: Add async audit pipeline with ring buffer. Phase 3: Implement Key Rotation Coordinator with multi-generation support. Phase 4: Add offline resilience with circuit breaker and secure enclave fallback. Each phase is independently testable and deployable.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/payment/gateway.ts — stateless request handling and validation","src/payment/processor.ts — payment aggregate orchestration","src/payment/repository.ts — encrypted payment persistence","src/crypto/encryption_service.ts — envelope encryption engine","src/crypto/key_cache.ts — in-memory DEK cache with TTL and generation tracking","src/crypto/kms_adapter.ts — interface + AWS KMS implementation","src/crypto/key_rotation_coordinator.ts — background rotation and re-encryption","src/audit/audit_pipeline.ts — ring buffer + consumer","src/audit/audit_event.ts — event schema (published language)","src/config/encryption_config.ts — key TTL, cache size, rotation schedule, offline policy"],"patterns_to_follow":"Hexagonal architecture: domain core (Payment, Encryption) has no infrastructure dependencies. Ports (Repository, KMS Adapter, Audit Pipeline) are interfaces. Adapters are swappable implementations. All sensitive operations go through Encryption Service — no direct crypto calls from business logic.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["DO NOT log plaintext payment data anywhere — audit logs record field names and access metadata, never field values","DO NOT store unwrapped DEKs to disk without encryption — cache is in-memory only, or encrypted with enclave-sealed key","DO NOT call KMS synchronously on the payment hot path after initial cache warm — always check cache first","DO NOT use a single DEK for all payments — use per-customer or per-session DEKs to limit blast radius of a single key compromise","DO NOT block payment processing on audit flush — audit is fire-and-forget into the ring buffer","DO NOT assume KMS latency is stable — P99 of KMS calls can spike to 500ms+, which is why caching is non-negotiable"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_constraints","description":"Requirement 4 (offline operation) conflicts with requirement 1 (customer-managed keys) under strict compliance interpretations. When KMS is unavailable, who controls the key? A pre-provisioned offline key is not truly customer-managed in real-time. Need customer/compliance sign-off on acceptable offline security posture.","options":["(A) Customer pre-authorizes a sealed offline key stored in local HSM — closest to CMK intent but requires customer involvement in provisioning","(B) Queue payments during KMS outage, process when KMS returns — meets CMK but violates 'must still process' requirement","(C) Accept degraded security posture during offline with compensating controls (enhanced monitoring, shorter key TTL, post-hoc audit)"],"recommendation":"Option A with Option C as fallback. Pre-provision customer-authorized offline key in secure enclave. Document the degraded posture. Require customer contractual sign-off. This is a business decision that architecture alone cannot resolve."}],"confidence":{"level":55,"high_confidence_areas":["Envelope encryption is the correct pattern for CMK + latency requirements","Async audit via ring buffer is necessary to meet P99 budget","Multi-generation key support is the right approach for zero-downtime rotation","KMS Adapter as anti-corruption layer is straightforward"],"low_confidence_areas":["Offline mode security posture — requires compliance/customer input to resolve the CMK conflict","Exact P99 budget feasibility — 50ms is achievable but tight; real numbers depend on infrastructure choices (DB, network, hardware)","Per-customer vs per-session DEK granularity — depends on customer isolation requirements not specified"],"assumptions":["Infrastructure supports mlock() and secure memory handling","External KMS supports envelope encryption (wrap/unwrap DEK operations)","Payment volume is high enough to justify the complexity (not a low-volume CRUD service)","PCI DSS compliance is required given the payment data sensitivity","The 50ms P99 is measured at the gateway boundary (not end-to-end including client network)"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Payment processing service with customer-managed encryption keys (CMK), 50ms P99 latency, full field-level audit, offline resilience, and zero-downtime key rotation. The core architectural challenge is that requirements 1, 2, and 4 are in direct tension: true CMK with remote KMS cannot meet 50ms P99 while also working offline. The design resolves this via a local key cache with encrypted Data Encryption Keys (DEKs) using an envelope encryption pattern, async audit pipeline,
